name: Leech Lake Area
short_description: High-elevation BLM rockhounding site in the northern California Coast Ranges where quartz, agate, and jasper specimens are found in moderate-slope terrain surrounded by mixed conifer forest.
description: Leech Lake Area offers rockhounders access to public BLM land in the remote mountains of Mendocino County, California. Situated at 6,113 feet elevation on moderate slopes, this site sits within the geologically complex Coast Ranges where metamorphic and igneous rocks host diverse quartz and feldspar minerals. The cooler, higher elevation means collecting is most comfortable during warmer months when snow melt has cleared access routes. This is a genuine backcountry experience - the remoteness means fewer crowds and excellent opportunities to explore multiple small creeks and exposed outcrops where quartz nodules, agate, and jasper specimens weather out of host rock. Expect to do significant hiking and scrambling to reach productive zones. The mixed conifer forest and moderate terrain demand sturdy boots and careful footing, but the location's accessibility via BLM roads and lack of permit requirements make it attractive for dedicated rockhounders willing to put in the effort.
history: The Leech Lake area sits within the Franciscan Complex and related metamorphic terranes that formed during the Mesozoic subduction of the Farallon Plate beneath North America. This tectonic activity created metamorphic rocks and quartz veins that now erode along slope drainages, releasing quartz, agate, and feldspar specimens that accumulate in stream beds and surface exposures characteristic of the northern California Coast Ranges.
tips: Search along small drainage channels and creeks on the moderate slopes - these naturally concentrate heavier specimens and expose fresh material from adjacent hillsides. Bring a geology hammer or chisel to test vein quartz and feldspar outcrops; strike gently to extract clean pieces. Look for translucent to milky quartz specimens, banded agate with warm earth tones, and feldspar crystals in weathered granite-adjacent rocks. The high elevation means cool nights even in summer - bring layers and plan your approach carefully since weather can deteriorate rapidly.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6,113 feet with moderate slopes in mixed conifer forest (Douglas fir, tanoak, madrone). Terrain is rocky and uneven with significant loose rock on hillsides and in drainage channels. Walking from parking areas to productive zones typically requires 0.5 to 2 miles of hiking over rough, rooty forest floor and rocky creek beds. Footing is challenging and requires solid boots with good ankle support. No developed trails; navigation by GPS or topo map recommended.
best_season: June - September. High elevation (6,113 ft) experiences snow and impassable conditions November through May. Even in summer, afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Coast Ranges. June and early September offer the most stable weather and best road access; July-August can have brief afternoon storms and higher heat during afternoon hours.
written_directions: From Willows (nearest significant town with services, approximately 85 miles south), take CA 162 west toward Mendocino County. Continue on forest roads into the Mendocino National Forest region. Access Leech Lake Area via Forest Service Road 18N01 (or alternate BLM access roads in the vicinity of GPS 39.917706, -123.086825). Roads are seasonal dirt/gravel; high-clearance vehicle strongly recommended, 4x4 preferred during wet months. Contact Bureau of Land Management - Ukiah Field Office for current road conditions and specific access routes, as forest roads in this region are subject to seasonal closures.
road_conditions: Seasonal dirt and gravel forest roads. High-clearance required; 4x4 strongly recommended, especially May-June and after rain. Roads may be closed or impassable during winter snow season.
parking_notes: No developed parking lot. Park at pullouts along forest access roads near trailheads leading to productive slopes and drainages. Space for 2-4 vehicles at typical pullout areas.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or destructive mining. Collect only loose specimens and hand-portable pieces from surface; do not blast, drill, or use power tools. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
25 lb per day limit per FLPMA
No commercial collection or sales
No motorized excavation
No blasting, drilling, or power tools
Hand collection and portable specimens only
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Willows (approximately 85 miles south) has gas, food, and water. Williams (smaller town, approximately 60 miles south) has limited services. No services within 40+ miles of the site itself.
accessibility_notes: Rough backcountry terrain suitable only for experienced hikers with proper equipment. Moderate to steep slopes, rocky footing, and loose rock throughout. Significant hiking (0.5 to 2+ miles) required from parking areas to productive collecting zones. Uneven forest floor with roots, rocks, and deadfall. No maintained trails, wheelchair inaccessible, not suitable for those with limited mobility. High elevation (6,113 ft) may affect those with altitude sensitivity.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding near Leech Lake in Mendocino County?
faq.answer: Leech Lake Area is known for quartz specimens, agate with banded patterns, jasper, and feldspar crystals. These minerals weather out of metamorphic and granitic rocks along slope drainages and creek beds at this high elevation site.

faq.question: Is there a permit required to rockhound at Leech Lake Area, California?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Leech Lake Area sits on public BLM land where recreational rockhounding is allowed for personal use only under FLPMA regulations. You are limited to 25 lbs per day and hand collection methods.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Leech Lake in California?
faq.answer: June through September is the collecting season. At 6,113 feet elevation, the area experiences snow and road closures November through May. June and early September offer the most stable weather; July and August can bring afternoon thunderstorms typical of the Coast Ranges.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Leech Lake Area and is it suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: This is an expert-level site requiring significant hiking (0.5 to 2+ miles) over rough, rocky terrain with loose footing and no maintained trails. It is not recommended for beginners, families with young children, or those with limited backcountry experience. A GPS unit and topo map are essential.

faq.question: What vehicle and tools do I need for collecting at Leech Lake Area?
faq.answer: A 4x4 high-clearance vehicle is strongly recommended to access seasonal forest roads. Bring a geology hammer or chisel for specimen extraction, sturdy boots with ankle support, GPS/topo map, water, and cold-weather layers. Cell service is nonexistent; inform someone of your location.

faq.question: Where exactly should I search for specimens at Leech Lake Area?
faq.answer: Focus your search along small drainage channels and creeks flowing down moderate slopes - these naturally concentrate heavier minerals and expose fresh material eroding from adjacent hillsides. Examine visible quartz veins and weathered feldspar-rich outcrops near the treeline and open slope areas.

meta_description: Find quartz, agate, and jasper on public BLM land near Leech Lake, Mendocino County. High-elevation rockhounding June-September. No permit required.
